title.zh: 卷四十二 張周趙任申屠傳
title.en: Volume 42: Zhang, Zhou, Zhao, Ren and Shentu
title.raw: 卷四十二 張周趙任申屠傳 Volume 42: Zhang, Zhou, Zhao, Ren and Shentu

sentences.zh: 張蒼，陽武人也，好書律曆。

translations.literal: Zhang Cang was a man of Yangwu; he loved books, statutes, and calendrical astronomy.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Cang was a native of Yangwu who devoted himself to books, law, and the calendar.

sentences.zh: 秦時為御史，主柱下方書。

translations.literal: In Qin times he was an imperial clerk, in charge of the documents kept beneath the pillar.
translations.idiomatic: Under the Qin he served as imperial clerk, supervising the archive of documents beneath the pillar.

sentences.zh: 有罪，亡歸。

translations.literal: Having committed an offense, he fled and returned home.
translations.idiomatic: He ran afoul of the law and fled home.

sentences.zh: 及沛公略地過陽武，蒼以客從攻南陽。

translations.literal: When the Lord of Pei seized territory and passed through Yangwu, Cang as a guest-retainer followed him to attack Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: When the Lord of Pei was expanding his holdings and marched through Yangwu, Zhang Cang joined him as a retainer and took part in the campaign against Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 蒼當斬，解衣伏質，身長大，肥白如瓠，時王陵見而怪其美士，乃言沛公，赦勿斬。

translations.literal: Cang was sentenced to beheading; he stripped and lay on the execution block. His body was tall and large, fat and white as a gourd. At that time Wang Ling saw him, marveled at this fine gentleman, and spoke to the Lord of Pei, who pardoned him from beheading.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Cang was condemned to death. He bared his torso and stretched out on the block—tall and heavyset, his skin pale and smooth as a gourd. Wang Ling happened to see him, struck by what an impressive man he was, and interceded with the Lord of Pei, who spared his life.

sentences.zh: 遂西入武關，至咸陽。

translations.literal: Thereupon they went west, entered Wu Pass, and reached Xianyang.
translations.idiomatic: They then marched west through Wu Pass into Xianyang.

sentences.zh: 沛公立為漢王，入漢中，還定三秦。

translations.literal: The Lord of Pei was established as King of Han; he entered Hanzhong and returned to pacify the Three Qin.
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Pei was made King of Han, withdrew into Hanzhong, and then fought his way back to subdue the Three Qin.

sentences.zh: 陳餘擊走常山王張耳，耳歸漢。

translations.literal: Chen Yu attacked and drove away the King of Changshan Zhang Er, and Er returned to Han.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Yu routed Zhang Er, the King of Changshan, who then came over to Han.

sentences.zh: 漢以蒼為常山守。

translations.literal: Han appointed Cang governor of Changshan.
translations.idiomatic: The Han court named Zhang Cang governor of Changshan.

sentences.zh: 從韓信擊趙，蒼得陳餘。

translations.literal: Following Han Xin in attacking Zhao, Cang captured Chen Yu.
translations.idiomatic: He followed Han Xin against Zhao and took Chen Yu prisoner.

sentences.zh: 趙地已平，漢王以蒼為代相，備邊冠。

translations.literal: When Zhao's territory was pacified, the King of Han made Cang minister of Dai, to prepare the border against incursions.
translations.idiomatic: Once Zhao had been secured, the King of Han appointed Zhang Cang minister of Dai to hold the northern frontier.

sentences.zh: 已而徙為趙相，相趙王耳。

translations.literal: Later he was transferred to be minister of Zhao, serving King Er of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: He was soon transferred to serve as Zhao's chancellor under King Zhang Er.

sentences.zh: 耳卒，相其子敖。

translations.literal: When Er died, he served his son Ao.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Er died, Zhang Cang became chancellor to his son Ao.

sentences.zh: 復徙相代。

translations.literal: Again he was transferred to minister Dai.
translations.idiomatic: He was moved once more to the chancellorship of Dai.

sentences.zh: 燕王臧荼反，蒼以代相從攻荼有功，封為北平侯，食邑千二百戶。

translations.literal: The King of Yan Zang Tu rebelled; Cang, as minister of Dai, followed in attacking Tu, meriting service, and was enfeoffed as marquis of Beiping with a fief of 1,200 households.
translations.idiomatic: When Zang Tu, king of Yan, rose in rebellion, Zhang Cang accompanied the campaign in his capacity as chancellor of Dai, distinguished himself, and was enfeoffed as marquis of Beiping with twelve hundred households.

sentences.zh: 遷為計相，一月，更以列侯為主計四歲。

translations.literal: He was promoted to counting minister; after one month, he was again made chief counter for four years as a full marquis.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted to counting minister; a month later he was reassigned, still as a full marquis, to serve four years as chief of the annual accounts.

sentences.zh: 是時，蕭何為相國，而蒼乃自秦時為柱下御史，明習天下圖書計籍，又善用算律曆，故令蒼以列侯居相府，領主郡國上計者。

translations.literal: At that time Xiao He was chancellor, and Cang had since Qin times been the pillar-clerk recorder; he was thoroughly versed in the empire's maps, documents, and registers, and moreover skilled at reckoning, statutes, and the calendar; therefore Cang was ordered, as a full marquis, to reside in the chancellor's office and oversee those who presented the annual accounts from the commanderies and kingdoms.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He was chancellor at the time, and Zhang Cang—who had been a pillar-clerk recorder under the Qin—knew the empire's maps, archives, and tax rolls inside out and was adept at calculation, law, and the calendar. The court therefore had him, still a full marquis, take quarters in the chancellor's compound and supervise the officials who received the year-end reports from every commandery and kingdom.

sentences.zh: 黥布反，漢立皇子長為淮南王，而蒼相之。

translations.literal: When Qing Bu rebelled, Han established the imperial son Chang as King of Huainan, and Cang served him as minister.
translations.idiomatic: When Qing Bu rebelled, the Han enthroned the imperial prince Liu Chang as king of Huainan, with Zhang Cang as his chancellor.

sentences.zh: 十四年，遷為御史大夫 〈(180 BCE)〉。

translations.literal: In the fourteenth year he was promoted to imperial counsellor (180 BCE).
translations.idiomatic: In the fourteenth year of his reign (180 BCE) he was raised to the rank of imperial counsellor.

sentences.zh: 正月甲午，御史大夫北平侯張蒼為丞相 〈(176 BCE)〉。

translations.literal: On the day jiawu of the first month, imperial counsellor and marquis of Beiping Zhang Cang became chancellor (176 BCE).
translations.idiomatic: On the jiawu day of the first month (176 BCE), Zhang Cang, marquis of Beiping and imperial counsellor, was appointed chancellor.

sentences.zh: 張蒼亦學律歷，以為非是，罷之。

translations.literal: Zhang Cang also studied statutes and the calendar; deeming it incorrect, he abolished it.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Cang had also mastered statutes and calendrical science; finding the prevailing scheme unsound, he set it aside.

sentences.zh: 其後黃龍見成紀，張蒼自黜，所欲論著不成。

translations.literal: Afterwards a yellow dragon appeared in Chengji; Zhang Cang demoted himself, and what he wished to compile did not come to completion.
translations.idiomatic: Later, when a yellow dragon was sighted at Chengji, Zhang Cang stepped down of his own accord, and the treatises he had hoped to finish never appeared.

sentences.zh: 而新垣平以望氣見，頗言正歷服色事，貴幸，後作亂，故孝文帝廢不復問。

translations.literal: Meanwhile Xinyuan Ping gained audience by observing qi-winds, spoke at length about correcting the calendar and court colors, became favored and honored, later rebelled; therefore Emperor Xiaowen cast it aside and did not inquire again.
translations.idiomatic: Xinyuan Ping had meanwhile won an audience by reading the clouds and airs, held forth on revising the calendar and ritual colors, rose to favor—and then turned traitor. Emperor Wen therefore dropped the whole matter and never reopened it.

sentences.zh: 置陽陵邑。

translations.literal: They established the town of Yangling.
translations.idiomatic: A settlement was founded at Yangling.

sentences.zh: 丞相北平侯張蒼卒 〈(151 BCE)〉。

translations.literal: Chancellor and marquis of Beiping Zhang Cang died (151 BCE).
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Cang, chancellor and marquis of Beiping, died in 151 BCE.

sentences.zh: 周昌者，沛人也。

translations.literal: Zhou Chang was a man of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Chang was a native of Pei.

sentences.zh: 其從兄苛，秦時皆為泗水卒史。

translations.literal: His cousin Ke; in Qin times both were clerks of the corps for Sishui.
translations.idiomatic: His cousin Zhou Ke had served with him under the Qin as clerks of the corps for the Sishui commandery.

sentences.zh: 及高祖起沛，擊破泗水守監，於是苛、昌以卒史從沛公，沛公以昌為職志，苛為客。

translations.literal: When the High Ancestor rose in Pei, struck and defeated the Sishui governor and supervisor, thereupon Ke and Chang followed the Lord of Pei as clerks of the corps; the Lord of Pei made Chang duty officer and Ke a guest-retainer.
translations.idiomatic: When the High Ancestor rose at Pei and overthrew the Sishui governor and his inspector, the Zhou brothers followed the Lord of Pei in their old clerkships; the Lord of Pei named Zhou Chang duty officer and Zhou Ke a guest-retainer.

sentences.zh: 從入關破秦。

translations.literal: They followed him into the passes and broke Qin.
translations.idiomatic: They accompanied him through the passes and helped bring down the Qin.

sentences.zh: 沛公立為漢王，以苛為御史大夫，昌為中尉。

translations.literal: The Lord of Pei was established as King of Han; he made Ke imperial counsellor and Chang commandant of the guard.
translations.idiomatic: When the Lord of Pei became King of Han, he appointed Zhou Ke imperial counsellor and Zhou Chang commandant of the guard.

sentences.zh: 漢三年，楚圍漢王滎陽急，漢王出去，而使苛守滎陽城。

translations.literal: In the third year of Han, Chu besieged the King of Han at Xingyang urgently; the King of Han went out but left Ke to hold Xingyang city.
translations.idiomatic: In the third year of Han, Chu forces had Xingyang in a stranglehold; the King of Han slipped away but left Zhou Ke to hold the city.

sentences.zh: 楚破滎陽城，欲令苛將，苛罵曰：「若趣降漢王！

translations.literal: When Chu broke Xingyang city, they wished to have Ke command; Ke cursed, saying, "You—hurry and surrender to the King of Han!
translations.idiomatic: When Chu took Xingyang, they tried to make Zhou Ke switch sides. He shouted back, "Surrender to the King of Han at once!

sentences.zh: 不然，今為虜矣！」

translations.literal: If not, you will now become captives!"
translations.idiomatic: If you refuse, you will be prisoners before the day is out!"

sentences.zh: 項羽怒，亨苛。

translations.literal: Xiang Yu in anger boiled Ke alive.
translations.idiomatic: Enraged, Xiang Yu had Zhou Ke executed in the cauldron.

sentences.zh: 漢王於是拜昌為御史大夫。

translations.literal: Thereupon the King of Han appointed Chang imperial counsellor.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han then named Zhou Chang imperial counsellor.

sentences.zh: 常從擊破項籍。

translations.literal: He constantly followed in striking and defeating Xiang Ji.
translations.idiomatic: He fought at the king's side until Xiang Yu was destroyed.

sentences.zh: 六年，與蕭、曹等俱封，為汾陰侯。

translations.literal: In the sixth year, together with Xiao, Cao, and others he was enfeoffed and made marquis of Fenyin.
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth year he was enfeoffed along with Xiao He, Cao Shen, and the rest, as marquis of Fenyin.

sentences.zh: 苛子成以父死事，封為高景侯。

translations.literal: Ke's son Cheng, because his father died in service, was enfeoffed as marquis of Gaojing.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Ke's son Cheng was enfeoffed as marquis of Gaojing in recognition of his father's sacrifice.

sentences.zh: 昌為人強力，敢直言，自蕭、曹等皆卑下之。

translations.literal: Chang was by nature forceful and dared speak plainly; even Xiao, Cao, and the rest held themselves beneath him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Chang was blunt and fearless; even Xiao He and Cao Shen treated him with deference.

sentences.zh: 昌嘗燕入奏事，高帝方擁戚姬，昌還走。

translations.literal: Chang once at ease entered to report affairs; the High Ancestor was embracing Lady Qi, and Chang turned and ran.
translations.idiomatic: Once, when Zhou Chang came in informally to report on business, he found the High Ancestor with Lady Qi in his arms and beat a hasty retreat.

sentences.zh: 高帝逐得，騎昌項，上問曰：「我何如主也？」

translations.literal: The High Ancestor chased and caught him, rode Chang's neck, and asked, "What sort of ruler am I?"
translations.idiomatic: The High Ancestor caught up, threw an arm over his shoulders like a rider's grip, and asked, "What kind of sovereign do you take me for?"

sentences.zh: 昌仰曰：「陛下即桀、紂之主也。」

translations.literal: Chang looked up and said, "Your Majesty is even a ruler like Jie or Zhou."
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Chang looked him in the eye and said, "You are a sovereign in the mold of Jie or Zhou—the last kings of Xia and Shang."

sentences.zh: 於是上笑之，然尤憚昌。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Lord laughed at him, yet all the more feared Chang.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor laughed—but from then on he was warier of Zhou Chang than ever.

sentences.zh: 及高帝欲廢太子，而立威姬子如意為太子，大臣固爭莫能得，上以留侯策止。

translations.literal: When the High Ancestor wished to abolish the heir apparent and establish the son of Lady Wei, Ruyi, as heir apparent, the great ministers firmly remonstrated but could not prevail, until the Lord stopped it with the Marquis of Liu's stratagem.
translations.idiomatic: When the High Ancestor wanted to set aside the heir apparent in favor of Lady Wei's son Ruyi, his senior ministers argued themselves hoarse to no avail, until he dropped the idea on the advice of the Marquis of Liu.

sentences.zh: 而昌庭爭之強，上問其說，昌為人吃，又盛怒，曰：「臣口不能言，然臣期期知其不可。

translations.literal: But Chang remonstrated forcefully in court; the Lord asked his reasoning. Chang stuttered, and moreover was in great anger, saying, "Your subject's mouth cannot speak, yet your subject knows it cannot be done.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Chang opposed him just as fiercely in open court. Asked to explain, he stammered with rage: "I—I cannot find the words, but I know in my bones this must not happen.

sentences.zh: 陛下欲廢太子，臣期期不奉詔。」

translations.literal: Your Majesty wishes to abolish the heir apparent; your subject will not accept the edict."
translations.idiomatic: If Your Majesty insists on deposing the heir, I—I will not carry out the edict."

sentences.zh: 上欣然而笑，即罷。

translations.literal: The Lord laughed with pleasure and desisted.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor broke into a relieved laugh and let the matter drop.

sentences.zh: 呂後側耳於東箱聽，見昌，為跪謝曰：「微君，太子幾廢。」

translations.literal: Empress Lü listened with her ear at the eastern corridor; when she saw Chang, she knelt in thanks and said, "Were it not for you, the heir would nearly have been abolished."
translations.idiomatic: Empress Lü had been listening from the eastern gallery. When she met Zhou Chang afterward, she knelt in gratitude and said, "Had it not been for you, the heir would have been lost."

sentences.zh: 是歲，戚姬子如意為趙王，年十歲，高祖憂萬歲之後不全也。

translations.literal: That year Lady Qi's son Ruyi became King of Zhao, aged ten, and the High Ancestor worried that after his ten-thousand-year span [death] he would not be kept whole.
translations.idiomatic: That same year Lady Qi's son Ruyi, only ten, was invested as king of Zhao, and the High Ancestor brooded over how to keep the boy safe after his own death.

sentences.zh: 趙堯為符璽御史，趙人方與公謂御史大夫周昌曰：「君之史趙堯年雖少，然奇士，君必異之，是且代君之位。」

translations.literal: Zhao Yao was clerk of the tallies and seals; a man of Zhao, the lord of Fangyu, said to imperial counsellor Zhou Chang, "Your clerk Zhao Yao, though young, is an extraordinary gentleman; you must treat him as unusual; he will replace your position."
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Yao served as clerk of tallies and seals. A Zhao native known as the lord of Fangyu said to Imperial Counsellor Zhou Chang, "That clerk of yours, Zhao Yao, is young, but he is no ordinary man. Mark my words—he will one day take your chair."

sentences.zh: 昌笑曰：「堯年少，刀筆吏耳，何至是乎！」

translations.literal: Chang laughed and said, "Yao is young, a brush-and-knife clerk only; how could it reach this!"
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Chang laughed. "Zhao Yao is a youngster—a mere scribal clerk. How could he rise that high?"

sentences.zh: 居頃之，堯侍高祖，高祖獨心不樂，悲歌，群臣不知上所以然。

translations.literal: After a short while, Yao attended the High Ancestor; the High Ancestor alone was unhappy in heart, sang sadly, and the assembled ministers did not know why the Lord was thus.
translations.idiomatic: Not long afterward Zhao Yao was attending the High Ancestor, who sat alone in gloom, humming a mournful tune while his ministers wondered what troubled him.

sentences.zh: 堯進請問曰：「陛下所為不樂，非以趙王年少，而戚夫人與呂後有隙，備萬歲之後而趙王不能自全乎？」

translations.literal: Yao advanced and asked, saying, "What Your Majesty does in being unhappy—is it not because the King of Zhao is young, and Lady Qi and Empress Lü have a rift, and you prepare for after your ten-thousand-year span that the King of Zhao cannot preserve himself?"
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Yao stepped forward and said softly, "Your Majesty's sorrow—is it not because the king of Zhao is still a child, because Lady Qi and the empress are at odds, and because you fear for his life once you are gone?"

sentences.zh: 高祖曰：「我私憂之，不知所出。」

translations.literal: The High Ancestor said, "I privately worry about it and do not know the way out."
translations.idiomatic: The High Ancestor replied, "It weighs on me privately, and I see no way through."

sentences.zh: 堯曰：「陛下獨為趙王置貴強相，及呂後、太子、群臣素所敬憚者乃可。」

translations.literal: Yao said, "Your Majesty alone for the King of Zhao should appoint a noble and strong minister, and it must be one whom Empress Lü, the heir apparent, and the assembled ministers have always respected and feared."
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Yao said, "Then invest Zhao with a chancellor who is both weighty and formidable—someone the empress, the heir, and the whole court already fear and respect."

sentences.zh: 高祖曰：「然。

translations.literal: The High Ancestor said, "So.
translations.idiomatic: The High Ancestor said, "Exactly.

sentences.zh: 吾念之欲如是，而群臣誰可者？」

translations.literal: I have thought to wish it thus, but among the assembled ministers who is suitable?"
translations.idiomatic: I have been turning that very thought over in my mind—but whom among my ministers can I send?"

sentences.zh: 堯曰：「御史大夫昌，其人堅忍伉直，自呂後、太子及大臣皆素嚴憚之。

translations.literal: Yao said, "Imperial counsellor Chang—his person is firm, enduring, upright, and blunt; from Empress Lü and the heir apparent down to the great ministers, all have always strictly feared him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Yao answered, "Imperial Counsellor Zhou Chang is stubborn, plain-spoken, and unbending. The empress, the heir, and every senior minister already tread softly around him.

sentences.zh: 獨昌可。」

translations.literal: Only Chang is suitable."
translations.idiomatic: He alone will do."

sentences.zh: 高祖曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The High Ancestor said, "Good."
translations.idiomatic: The High Ancestor said, "Good."

sentences.zh: 於是召昌謂曰：「吾固欲煩公，公強為我相趙。」

translations.literal: Thereupon he summoned Chang and said, "I indeed wish to trouble you; you must force yourself to be minister of Zhao for me."
translations.idiomatic: He summoned Zhou Chang and said, "I hate to impose on you, but I need you to take the chancellorship of Zhao."

sentences.zh: 昌泣曰：「臣初起從陛下，陛下獨奈何中道而棄之于諸侯乎？」

translations.literal: Chang wept and said, "Your subject from the first rose following Your Majesty; how can Your Majesty alone midway abandon him among the feudal lords?"
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Chang wept. "I have followed Your Majesty since the very beginning. How can you cast me off midway among the regional kings?"

sentences.zh: 高祖曰：「吾極知其左遷，然吾私憂趙，念非公無可者。

translations.literal: The High Ancestor said, "I extremely know it is a demotion, yet I privately worry for Zhao, and thinking, there is none suitable but you.
translations.idiomatic: The High Ancestor said, "I know full well this is a step down for you. My heart is with Zhao, and no one but you will serve.

sentences.zh: 公不得已強行！」

translations.literal: You have no choice but to force yourself to go!"
translations.idiomatic: You must go, however hard it is!"

sentences.zh: 於是徙御史大夫昌為趙相。

translations.literal: Thereupon imperial counsellor Chang was transferred to be minister of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial Counsellor Zhou Chang was therefore transferred to the chancellorship of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 既行久之，高祖持御史大夫印弄之，曰：「誰可以為御史大夫者？」

translations.literal: After he had gone a long time, the High Ancestor took the imperial counsellor's seal and toyed with it, saying, "Who can be imperial counsellor?"
translations.idiomatic: Some time after Zhou Chang had left, the High Ancestor turned the imperial counsellor's seal over in his hands and asked, "Who is fit to be imperial counsellor now?"

sentences.zh: 孰視堯曰：「無以易堯。」

translations.literal: He stared at Yao and said, "None can replace Yao."
translations.idiomatic: He fixed his gaze on Zhao Yao and said, "No one else will do."

sentences.zh: 遂拜堯為御史大夫。

translations.literal: Thereupon he appointed Yao imperial counsellor.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Yao was appointed imperial counsellor on the spot.

sentences.zh: 堯亦前有軍功食邑，及以御史大夫從擊陳豨有功，封為江邑侯。

translations.literal: Yao also previously had military merit and a fief, and as imperial counsellor followed in attacking Chen Xi, meriting service, and was enfeoffed as marquis of Jiangyi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Yao already held a fief for earlier military service; as imperial counsellor he campaigned against Chen Xi with distinction and was enfeoffed as marquis of Jiangyi.

sentences.zh: 高祖崩，太后使使召趙王，其相昌令王稱疾不行。

translations.literal: When the High Ancestor died, the empress dowager sent envoys to summon the King of Zhao; his minister Chang ordered the king to claim illness and not go.
translations.idiomatic: After the High Ancestor's death, the empress dowager sent for the king of Zhao. Chancellor Zhou Chang had the young king plead illness and refuse to travel.

sentences.zh: 使者三反，昌曰：「高帝屬臣趙王，王年少，竊聞太后怨戚夫人，欲召趙王並誅之。

translations.literal: The envoys returned three times; Chang said, "The High Emperor entrusted the King of Zhao to your subject; the king is young; I privately hear the empress dowager resents Lady Qi and wishes to summon the King of Zhao together to execute them.
translations.idiomatic: The envoys came back three times. Zhou Chang replied, "The late emperor left the king of Zhao in my care. The boy is young, and word has reached me that the empress dowager bears a grudge against Lady Qi and means to summon the king of Zhao so she can destroy them together.

sentences.zh: 臣不敢遣王，王且亦疾，不能奉詔。」

translations.literal: Your subject dares not send the king; the king moreover is also ill and cannot obey the edict."
translations.idiomatic: I dare not send him on his way, and in any case he is unwell and cannot obey the summons."

sentences.zh: 太后怒，乃使使召趙相。

translations.literal: The empress dowager was angry and thereupon sent envoys to summon the minister of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Furious, the empress dowager sent for the chancellor of Zhao instead.

sentences.zh: 相至，謁太后，太后罵昌曰：「爾不知我之怨戚氏乎？

translations.literal: When the minister arrived, he visited the empress dowager; the empress dowager cursed Chang, saying, "Do you not know my resentment toward the Qi clan?
translations.idiomatic: When he arrived at court and was admitted to her presence, she rounded on Zhou Chang: "Do you not know how I hate the Qi house?

sentences.zh: 而不遣趙王！」

translations.literal: Yet you do not send the King of Zhao!"
translations.idiomatic: Yet you still would not send me the king of Zhao!"

sentences.zh: 昌既被征，高後使使召趙王。

translations.literal: When Chang had already been summoned, Empress Gao sent envoys to summon the King of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Once Zhou Chang had been called away, Empress Gao sent again for the king of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 王果來，至長安月餘，見鴆殺。

translations.literal: The king indeed came; after reaching Chang'an more than a month, he was seen killed by poisoned wine.
translations.idiomatic: The boy came. Within little more than a month of his arrival in Chang'an he was dead—poisoned.

sentences.zh: 昌謝病不朝見，三歲而薨，諡曰悼侯。

translations.literal: Chang declined illness and did not attend court audience; after three years he died, posthumous title marquis Dao.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Chang pleaded illness and stayed away from court. He died three years later and was given the posthumous title Dao—"the Lamented Marquis."

sentences.zh: 傳子至孫意，有罪，國除。

translations.literal: It passed to the son down to the grandson Yi, who committed an offense, and the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: The title passed to his son and then to his grandson Yi, who forfeited the fief for a crime.

sentences.zh: 景帝復封昌孫左車為安陽侯，有罪，國除。

translations.literal: Emperor Jing again enfeoffed Chang's grandson Zuoche as marquis of Anyang, who committed an offense, and the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing later restored the line, enfeoffing Zhou Chang's grandson Zuoche as marquis of Anyang, but that fief, too, was stripped after a conviction.

sentences.zh: 初，趙堯既代周昌為御史大夫，高祖崩，事惠帝終世。

translations.literal: At first, when Zhao Yao had replaced Zhou Chang as imperial counsellor, the High Ancestor died, and he served Emperor Hui to the end of his reign.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Yao had succeeded Zhou Chang as imperial counsellor before the High Ancestor died, and he served out Emperor Hui's entire reign.

sentences.zh: 高後元年，怨堯前定趙王如意之畫，乃抵堯罪，以廣阿侯任敖為御史大夫。

translations.literal: In the first year of Empress Gao, resenting Yao's earlier settling the plan for King of Zhao Ruyi, she struck Yao with a crime and made marquis of Guang'a Ren Ao imperial counsellor.
translations.idiomatic: In the first year of Empress Gao's reign she remembered how Zhao Yao had engineered the plan to protect Prince Ruyi as king of Zhao, found a charge against him, and replaced him with Ren Ao, marquis of Guang'a, as imperial counsellor.

sentences.zh: 初任敖免，平陽侯曹窋代敖為御史大夫。

translations.literal: At first when Ren Ao was dismissed, marquis of Pingyang Cao Zhu replaced Ao as imperial counsellor.
translations.idiomatic: When Ren Ao was removed from office, Cao Zhu, marquis of Pingyang, succeeded him as imperial counsellor.

sentences.zh: 高後崩，與大臣共誅諸呂。

translations.literal: When Empress Gao died, together with the great ministers he jointly executed the various Lüs.
translations.idiomatic: After Empress Gao's death he joined the senior ministers in wiping out the Lü clan.

sentences.zh: 後坐事免，以淮南相張蒼為御史大夫。

translations.literal: Later he sat for an affair and was dismissed; Huainan minister Zhang Cang was made imperial counsellor.
translations.idiomatic: He was later dismissed for an offense, and Zhang Cang, chancellor of Huainan, was named imperial counsellor in his place.

sentences.zh: 蒼來絳侯等尊立孝文皇帝，四年，代灌嬰為丞相。

translations.literal: Cang came; Marquis of Jiang and others honored and established Emperor Xiaowen; in the fourth year he replaced Guan Ying as chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Cang had arrived with the Marquis of Jiang and the others who raised Emperor Wen to the throne; in the fourth year of that reign he succeeded Guan Ying as chancellor.

sentences.zh: 漢興二十餘年，天下初定，公卿皆軍吏。

translations.literal: Han had risen more than twenty years; the empire was first settled; dukes and ministers were all military clerks.
translations.idiomatic: More than twenty years had passed since the founding of Han; the realm was only beginning to settle, and most of the men at the summit of government were veterans who had risen from the ranks.

sentences.zh: 蒼為計相時，緒正律曆。

translations.literal: When Cang was counting minister, he ordered and corrected statutes and the calendar.
translations.idiomatic: While Zhang Cang held the office of counting minister, he set the statutes and calendar in order.

sentences.zh: 以高祖十月始至霸上，故因秦時本十月為歲首，不革。

translations.literal: Because the High Ancestor in the tenth month first reached Bashang, therefore following Qin times they fundamentally took the tenth month as year beginning, not changing it.
translations.idiomatic: The High Ancestor had first reached Bashang in the tenth month, so the Han kept the Qin practice of reckoning the new year from the tenth month and did not alter it.

sentences.zh: 推五德之運，以為漢當水德之時，上黑如故。

translations.literal: He inferred the movement of the five powers; he thought Han was at the time of the water power, and upward black remained as before.
translations.idiomatic: He traced the cycle of the Five Phases and concluded that Han stood under the power of Water; court ritual therefore continued to favor black, as under the Qin.

sentences.zh: 吹律調樂，入之音聲，及以比定律令。

translations.literal: He blew pitch-pipes to adjust music, entered into sounds and tones, and thereby compared and fixed statutes and orders.
translations.idiomatic: He matched pitch-pipes to tune the court music, aligned the scales, and on that basis harmonized the statutes and regulations.

sentences.zh: 若百工，天下作程品。

translations.literal: As for the hundred crafts, the empire made models and grades.
translations.idiomatic: For every craft under heaven he established standards and grades of workmanship.

sentences.zh: 至於為丞相，卒就之。

translations.literal: As for becoming chancellor, in the end he completed it.
translations.idiomatic: By the time he rose to chancellor, he had brought the whole program to completion.

sentences.zh: 故漢家言律曆者本張蒼。

translations.literal: Therefore those of Han who spoke of statutes and the calendar took Zhang Cang as root.
translations.idiomatic: Henceforth, whenever the Han court spoke of law and the calendar, it traced the authority back to Zhang Cang.

sentences.zh: 蒼凡好書，無所不觀，無所不通，而尤邃律曆。

translations.literal: Cang in general loved books; there was nothing he did not read, nothing he did not penetrate, and especially he was deep in statutes and the calendar.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Cang loved books in general—he read widely, missed little, and went deepest of all into law and calendrical science.

sentences.zh: 蒼德安國侯王陵，及貴，父事陵。

translations.literal: Cang owed virtue to Marquis of Anguo Wang Ling, and when he became noble, he served Ling as a father.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Cang never forgot that Wang Ling, marquis of Anguo, had saved his life; once he was powerful, he treated Wang Ling with the deference a son owes a father.

sentences.zh: 陵死後，蒼為丞相，洗沐，常先朝陵夫人上食，然後敢歸家。

translations.literal: After Ling died, when Cang was chancellor, on bath days he always first visited Lady Ling to present food, then dared return home.
translations.idiomatic: After Wang Ling died, Zhang Cang—by then chancellor—would on his days of rest call first on Lady Wang to bring her provisions before he would think of going home.

sentences.zh: 初蒼父長不滿五尺，蒼長八尺餘，蒼子復長八尺，及孫類長六尺餘。

translations.literal: At first Cang's father Chang was not full five chi tall; Cang was more than eight chi tall; Cang's son again was eight chi tall; reaching the grandson Lei was more than six chi tall.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Cang's father had stood under five feet; Zhang Cang himself towered at well over eight feet; his son matched that height; by the grandson Lei the line had shrunk to a little over six feet.

sentences.zh: 蒼免相後，口中無齒，食乳，女子為乳母。

translations.literal: After Cang was dismissed from chancellor, he had no teeth in his mouth; he drank milk; women served as wet-nurses.
translations.idiomatic: After Zhang Cang left the chancellorship he lost his teeth and lived on human milk, supplied by a household of wet nurses.

sentences.zh: 妻妾以百數，嘗孕者不復幸。

translations.literal: Wives and concubines were in the hundreds; those who had once been pregnant he did not again favor.
translations.idiomatic: He kept more than a hundred wives and concubines but never again visited any woman who had once borne him a child.

sentences.zh: 年百餘歲乃卒。

translations.literal: At more than a hundred years of age he then died.
translations.idiomatic: He lived past the age of a hundred before he died.

sentences.zh: 著書十八篇，言陰陽律曆事。

translations.literal: He composed writings in eighteen pieces, speaking of yin-yang, statutes, and calendrical matters.
translations.idiomatic: He left eighteen treatises on yin and yang, law, and the calendar.

sentences.zh: 申屠嘉

translations.literal: Shentu Jia (section heading).
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Jia

sentences.zh: 申屠嘉，梁人也。

translations.literal: Shentu Jia was a man of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Jia was a native of Liang.

sentences.zh: 以材官蹶張從高帝擊項籍，遷為隊率。

translations.literal: As a strong-arm crossbowman he followed the High Ancestor in attacking Xiang Ji and was promoted to platoon leader.
translations.idiomatic: He served as a strong-arm crossbowman under the High Ancestor in the war against Xiang Yu and rose to platoon leader.

sentences.zh: 從擊黥布，為都尉。

translations.literal: He followed in attacking Qing Bu and became commandant.
translations.idiomatic: He campaigned against Qing Bu and was named commandant.

sentences.zh: 孝惠時，為淮陽守。

translations.literal: In Emperor Xiaohui's time, he was governor of Huaiyang.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Hui he was governor of Huaiyang.

sentences.zh: 孝文元年，舉故以二千石從高祖者，悉以為關內侯，食邑二十四人，而嘉食邑五百戶。

translations.literal: In the first year of Emperor Xiaowen, those formerly at two-thousand-dan rank who had followed the High Ancestor were all raised to nobles within the passes; twenty-four received fiefs, and Jia had a fief of five hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: In the first year of Emperor Wen's reign, every former official at the two-thousand-dan level who had served the High Ancestor was ennobled as a noble within the passes; twenty-four were granted revenue from estates, among them Jia, with five hundred households.

sentences.zh: 十六年，遷為御史大夫。

translations.literal: In the sixteenth year he was promoted to imperial counsellor.
translations.idiomatic: In the sixteenth year of that reign he was promoted to imperial counsellor.

sentences.zh: 張蒼免相，文帝以皇后弟竇廣國賢有行，欲相之，曰：「恐天下以吾私廣國。」

translations.literal: Zhang Cang was dismissed from chancellor; Emperor Wen, because the empress's younger brother Dou Guangguo was worthy and had conduct, wished to make him chancellor, saying, "I fear the empire will think I favor Guangguo privately."
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Cang left the chancellorship, Emperor Wen wished to appoint the empress's brother Dou Guangguo—virtuous and well regarded—to the post, but said, "I am afraid the realm would think I am playing favorites for Guangguo."

sentences.zh: 久念不可，而高帝時大臣余見無可者，乃以御史大夫嘉為丞相，因故邑封為故安侯。

translations.literal: Long pondering, it could not be done; yet among the great ministers of the High Emperor's time who remained, he saw none suitable; thereupon he made imperial counsellor Jia chancellor, and on account of his former fief enfeoffed him as marquis of Gu'an.
translations.idiomatic: After long reflection he gave the idea up, and among the surviving senior ministers who had served the founder he could find no one equal to the task. He therefore elevated Imperial Counsellor Shentu Jia to chancellor and, building on the old enfeoffment, created him marquis of Gu'an.

sentences.zh: 嘉為人廉直，門不受私謁。

translations.literal: Jia as a person was incorrupt and upright; his gate did not accept private visits.
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Jia was honest and blunt; he would not receive private callers at his gate.

sentences.zh: 是時，太中大夫鄧通方愛幸，賞賜累巨萬。

translations.literal: At that time Grandee of the Palace Deng Tong was just then loved and favored; rewards and gifts accumulated to tens of thousands in gold.
translations.idiomatic: At that time Deng Tong, a grandee of the palace, stood high in the emperor's favor, and the gifts heaped on him ran into tens of thousands in cash.

sentences.zh: 文帝常燕飲通家，其寵如是。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen often at ease drank at Tong's house; his favor was thus.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen often dropped in informally to drink at Deng Tong's house; such was the extent of his favor.

sentences.zh: 是時，嘉入朝而通居上旁，有怠慢之禮。

translations.literal: At that time, when Jia entered court, Tong sat beside the Lord and had negligent ritual demeanor.
translations.idiomatic: Once, when Shentu Jia came to court, Deng Tong was lounging at the emperor's side with a slovenly disregard for protocol.

sentences.zh: 嘉奏事畢，因言曰：「陛下幸愛群臣則富貴之，至於朝廷之禮，不可以不肅！」

translations.literal: When Jia finished presenting affairs, he thereupon said, "If Your Majesty in favor loves the assembled ministers, then enrich and ennoble them; as for the ritual of the court, it cannot but be solemn!"
translations.idiomatic: When Shentu Jia had finished his report, he added, "If Your Majesty wishes to show love to your ministers, wealth and rank are the way to do it—but the dignity of the court must be kept inviolate."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「君勿言，吾私之。」

translations.literal: The Lord said, "You, do not speak; I favor him privately."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "Say no more; he is my private favorite."

sentences.zh: 罷朝坐府中，嘉為檄召通詣丞相府，不來，且斬通。

translations.literal: Court dismissed, sitting in the office, Jia drafted a summons calling Tong to come to the chancellor's office; if he did not come, he would behead Tong.
translations.idiomatic: After court Shentu Jia returned to his compound and issued a written summons ordering Deng Tong to report to the chancellor's residence, warning that if he failed to appear he would be executed.

sentences.zh: 通恐，入言上。

translations.literal: Tong was afraid and entered to speak to the Lord.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Tong was terrified and ran to the emperor.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「汝第往，吾今使人召若。」

translations.literal: The Lord said, "You only go; I now will send someone to summon you."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor told him, "Go when you are summoned; I will send for you in good time."

sentences.zh: 通至丞相府，免冠，徒跣，頓首謝嘉。

translations.literal: When Tong reached the chancellor's office, he removed cap, went barefoot, and knocked his head in apology to Jia.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Tong presented himself at the chancellor's gate bareheaded and barefoot, kowtowing in apology to Shentu Jia.

sentences.zh: 嘉坐自如，弗為禮，責曰：「夫朝廷者，高皇帝之朝廷也，通小臣，戲殿上，大不敬，當斬。

translations.literal: Jia sat at ease, did not perform ritual for him, and rebuked, saying, "As for the court, it is the High Emperor's court; Tong is a petty minister, playing on the hall—great disrespect; he should be beheaded.
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Jia remained seated and did not return the courtesy. "This court," he said, "is the court of the High Emperor. Deng Tong is a minor official who has made a mockery of the throne—a capital offense.

sentences.zh: 史今行斬之！」

translations.literal: Scribe—now carry out beheading him!"
translations.idiomatic: Officers, carry out the sentence and behead him!"

sentences.zh: 通頓首，首盡出血，不解。

translations.literal: Tong knocked his head; his head was entirely bleeding out, and he was not released.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Tong knocked his head on the floor until the blood ran, but Shentu Jia would not relent.

sentences.zh: 上度丞相已困通，使使持節召通，而謝丞相：「此語弄臣，君釋之。」

translations.literal: The Lord estimated the chancellor had already cornered Tong; he sent an envoy bearing credentials to summon Tong, and apologized to the chancellor, "This is my jesting minister; you, release him."
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor judged that the chancellor had Deng Tong sufficiently frightened, he dispatched an envoy with imperial credentials to recall him and sent word to Shentu Jia: "He is my household fool; let him go."

sentences.zh: 鄧通既至，為上泣曰：「丞相幾殺臣。」

translations.literal: When Deng Tong had arrived, he wept to the Lord, saying, "The chancellor nearly killed your subject."
translations.idiomatic: When Deng Tong reached the palace, he wept before the emperor and said, "The chancellor came within a hair of killing me."

sentences.zh: 嘉為丞相五歲，文帝崩，孝景即位。

translations.literal: Jia was chancellor five years; Emperor Wen died, and Emperor Xiaojing ascended.
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Jia had been chancellor five years when Emperor Wen died and Emperor Jing came to the throne.

sentences.zh: 二年，晁錯為內史，貴幸用事，諸法令多所請變更，議以適罰侵削諸侯，而丞相嘉自絀所言不用，疾錯。

translations.literal: In the second year, Chao Cuo was inner scribe, noble and favored and wielding power; on many statutes and orders he requested changes, deliberating to use fines and penalties to encroach and diminish the feudal lords, while Chancellor Jia himself was thwarted that his words were not used, and hated Cuo.
translations.idiomatic: In the second year of the reign Chao Cuo held the post of inner scribe; favored and powerful, he pushed through wholesale revisions of the law and urged fines and legal pressure to whittle away the princes' domains. Chancellor Shentu Jia found his own counsel ignored and came to detest Chao Cuo.

sentences.zh: 錯為內史，門東出，不便，更穿一門，南出。

translations.literal: Cuo as inner scribe had his gate exit east—it was inconvenient; he further bored a gate, exiting south.
translations.idiomatic: As inner scribe, Chao Cuo's official gate opened inconveniently to the east, so he opened a second gate to the south.

sentences.zh: 南出者，太上皇廟堧垣也。

translations.literal: What exited south was the Grand Supreme Emperor temple's shielding wall.
translations.idiomatic: The southern opening cut through the sacred boundary wall of the Grand Supreme Emperor's temple.

sentences.zh: 嘉聞錯穿宗廟垣，為奏請誅錯。

translations.literal: When Jia heard Cuo had bored through the ancestral temple wall, he made a memorial requesting execution of Cuo.
translations.idiomatic: When Shentu Jia learned that Chao Cuo had breached a wall of the imperial temple precinct, he memorialized for Chao Cuo's death.

sentences.zh: 客有語錯，錯恐，夜入宮上謁，自歸上。

translations.literal: A guest informed Cuo; Cuo was afraid, entered the palace by night to present a visit, and submitted himself to the Lord.
translations.idiomatic: A client warned Chao Cuo, who in panic went to the palace that night, sought an audience, and placed himself at the emperor's mercy.

sentences.zh: 至朝，嘉請誅內史錯。

translations.literal: When court came, Jia requested execution of Inner Scribe Cuo.
translations.idiomatic: At the next court session Shentu Jia demanded the execution of Inner Scribe Chao Cuo.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「錯所穿非真廟垣，乃外堧垣，故冗官居其中，且又我使為之，錯無罪。」

translations.literal: The Lord said, "What Cuo bored through was not the true temple wall; it was the outer shielding wall, where former supernumerary officials dwelt within, and moreover I ordered him to do it; Cuo has no guilt."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor replied, "Chao Cuo did not breach the inner temple wall but the outer precinct wall, where retired supernumerary officials were quartered—and I told him to do it. He is guilty of nothing."

sentences.zh: 罷朝，嘉謂長史曰：「吾悔不先斬錯乃請之，為錯所賣！」

translations.literal: Court dismissed, Jia said to the chief clerk, "I regret not first beheading Cuo and then requesting it—I have been sold by Cuo!"
translations.idiomatic: After court Shentu Jia told his chief clerk, "I should have cut Chao Cuo's throat before I ever brought the matter to the throne—he has made a fool of me."

sentences.zh: 至舍，因嘔血而死。

translations.literal: When he reached his lodging, thereupon he vomited blood and died.
translations.idiomatic: He reached home, vomited blood, and died.

sentences.zh: 諡曰節侯。

translations.literal: Posthumous title was marquis Jie.
translations.idiomatic: His posthumous title was Jie—"the Steadfast Marquis."

sentences.zh: 傳子至孫臾，有罪，國除。

translations.literal: It passed to the son down to the grandson Yu, who committed an offense, and the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: The title passed to his son and then to his grandson Yu, who lost the fief for a crime.

sentences.zh: 自嘉死後，開封侯陶青、桃侯劉舍及武帝時柏至侯許昌、平棘侯薛澤、武強侯莊青翟、商陵侯趙周，皆以列侯繼踵，娖娖廉謹，為丞相備員而已，無所能發明功名著於世者。

translations.literal: From after Jia's death, Marquis of Kaifeng Tao Qing, Marquis of Tao Liu She, and in Emperor Wu's time Marquis of Bozhi Xu Chang, Marquis of Pingji Xue Ze, Marquis of Wuqiang Zhuang Qingdi, Marquis of Shangling Zhao Zhou—all followed one another as full marquises, cautious and careful, incorrupt and prudent, serving as chancellor reserve personnel only, none able to develop and clarify merit and fame manifest in the world.
translations.idiomatic: After Shentu Jia's death came Tao Qing of Kaifeng and Liu She of Tao, and under Emperor Wu Xu Chang of Bozhi, Xue Ze of Pingji, Zhuang Qingdi of Wuqiang, and Zhao Zhou of Shangling—each a full marquis who stepped into the chancellorship in turn. They were honest, cautious placeholders, none of whom left a mark on the age.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：張蒼文好律曆，為漢名相，而專遵用奉之《顓頊曆》，何哉？

translations.literal: The appraisal says: Zhang Cang was cultured and loved statutes and the calendar, and was a famous Han chancellor, yet he solely followed and used the Zhuanxu Calendar offered in submission—why?
translations.idiomatic: The historian's appraisal: Zhang Cang was learned in law and the calendar and ranked among the Han's great chancellors—yet he clung exclusively to the Zhuanxu calendar the Qin had adopted. How could that be?

sentences.zh: 周昌，木強人也。

translations.literal: Zhou Chang was a wooden, forceful man.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Chang was a blunt, obstinate man.

sentences.zh: 任敖以舊德用。

translations.literal: Ren Ao was employed because of old virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Ren Ao rose on the strength of long-standing loyalty.

sentences.zh: 申屠嘉可謂剛毅守節，然無術學，殆與蕭、曹、陳平異矣。

translations.literal: Shentu Jia can be called firm and resolute, guarding integrity, yet without methods and learning—probably he differed from Xiao, Cao, and Chen Ping.
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Jia was resolute and held fast to principle, but he lacked the strategic breadth of learning that set Xiao He, Cao Shen, and Chen Ping apart.
